1:
"And it came to pass at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed: and, behold, he stood by the river." The witnesses note the time, two full years after, and Pharaoh's dream by the river. Gill notes it is not clear, as Aben Ezra observes, whence these years are reckoned, whether from Joseph's being put into prison or from the time of the butler's dream; most take it from the latter. The witnesses note that, two full years later, Pharaoh dreamed, standing by the river; the appointed time arrived, God now beginning Joseph's deliverance through a king's dream — that, after long delay, God's hour strikes: "at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed: and, behold, he stood by the river"; the witnesses note the "two full years" — that, after the chief butler had forgotten Joseph (40:23), two whole years passed in which Joseph lay still in prison, waiting; and now, at "the end" of them, God's time being come, the means of his release is set in motion; the witnesses note the place, "by the river" (the Nile), the very source of Egypt's plenty and famine, fitly the scene of the dream that concerned them; the witnesses note the providence in the timing, that God let Joseph wait the full appointed term, neither sooner nor later, and then moved a heathen king by a dream to bring about his servant's exaltation; so that, at the set time, Pharaoh dreams by the river; at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed. The witnesses note at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed. The witnesses note and, behold, he stood by the river.

The witnesses note Pharaoh's dream by the river, at the end of two full years — Joseph having waited the long appointed term in prison; God's hour now striking, moving a heathen king by a dream to bring about his servant's release.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointed time of Pharaoh's dream. The lesson the expositors draw is that, after Joseph's two-year wait, God's set time came, and he moved Pharaoh by a dream to begin Joseph's deliverance. The witnesses note the two full years: "at the end of two full years" — Gill notes from the butler's dream; the two full years; the the wait; the from the butler; the the prison; the still. The expositors observe the Joseph waited: Joseph lay still in prison, waiting; the Joseph waited; the lay still; the in prison; the the wait; the forgotten. The witnesses press the Pharaoh dreamed: "that Pharaoh dreamed" — God moves the king; the Pharaoh dreamed; the the king; the the dream; the moved; the heathen. The expositors draw the lesson on the by the river: "he stood by the river" — the Nile, Egypt's source; the by the river; the the Nile; the Egypt's source; the plenty; the famine. The witnesses observe the the appointed time: God's appointed time come; the the appointed time; the God's time; the the hour; the come; the set. The expositors note the the providence: the providence in the timing; the the providence; the the timing; the neither sooner; the nor later; the moved. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the at the end of two full years that Pharaoh dreamed, the and behold he stood by the river, and the appointed time. The verse thus records the appointed time of Pharaoh's dream — teaching that, after Joseph's wait, God's set time came and he moved Pharaoh by a dream.
2:
"And, behold, there came up out of the river seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed; and they fed in a meadow." The witnesses note the seven fat, well-favoured kine coming up out of the river. Gill notes seven cows or heifers, sleek, fat, and plump, goodly to look at; they seemed in the dream to come out of the river, because the fruitfulness of Egypt depends on the overflowing of the Nile. The witnesses note seven well-favoured and fatfleshed kine coming up out of the river and feeding in a meadow; the first part of the dream, the fat kine that figured the years of plenty — that the vision begins with abundance: "there came up out of the river seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed; and they fed in a meadow"; the witnesses note the imagery — "seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed," sleek and goodly cattle, coming "up out of the river" and feeding in a rich "meadow"; the witnesses note the fitness of the figures the commentators draw — that the kine rose out of "the river" (the Nile) because Egypt's fruitfulness depended wholly on the river's overflow, and the cow was a fit emblem of the tilling and the plenty of the land; the witnesses note that these fat kine, as the interpretation would show, figured the seven coming years of great plenty in Egypt; the witnesses note that God chose images drawn from Egypt's own life to foreshow what he would do to Egypt; so that the seven fat kine rise, the figure of plenty; seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed; and they fed in a meadow. The witnesses note there came up out of the river seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed. The witnesses note and they fed in a meadow.

The witnesses note the seven fat, well-favoured kine rising from the Nile — the figure of the coming years of plenty; God drawing the images from Egypt's own life, the river on whose overflow her fruitfulness depended.

So the witnesses make the verse the fat kine, figure of plenty. The lesson the expositors draw is that the seven fat kine rising from the river figured the seven coming years of plenty, the images drawn from Egypt's own life. The witnesses note the seven fat kine: "seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed" — Gill notes sleek and goodly; the seven fat kine; the sleek; the goodly; the fat; the well-favoured. The expositors observe the out of the river: "came up out of the river" — from the Nile; the out of the river; the the Nile; the the river; the overflow; the fruitfulness. The witnesses press the fed in a meadow: "they fed in a meadow" — a rich pasture; the fed in a meadow; the the meadow; the rich; the pasture; the plenty. The expositors draw the lesson on the figure of plenty: the figure of the years of plenty; the figure of plenty; the the plenty; the seven years; the abundance; the figured. The witnesses observe the Egypt's life: images from Egypt's own life; the Egypt's life; the the river; the the cow; the the land; the drawn. The expositors note the foreshow to Egypt: to foreshow what God would do; the foreshow to Egypt; the foreshow; the God's doing; the to Egypt; the the dream. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the there came up out of the river seven well favoured kine and fatfleshed, the and they fed in a meadow, and the fat kine. The verse thus records the fat kine, figure of plenty — teaching that the seven fat kine figured the coming years of plenty, the images from Egypt's own life.
3:
"And, behold, seven other kine came up after them out of the river, ill favoured and leanfleshed; and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river." The witnesses note the seven lean, ill-favoured kine coming up after the fat. Gill notes these were thin and haggard, their bones sticking out, having scarce any flesh upon them, making a wretched figure. The witnesses note seven ill-favoured and leanfleshed kine coming up after the fat ones and standing by them on the river's brink; the second part of the dream, the lean kine that figured the years of famine — that a fearful contrast appears: "seven other kine came up after them out of the river, ill favoured and leanfleshed; and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river"; the witnesses note the dreadful imagery — "seven other kine... ill favoured and leanfleshed," gaunt and starved, "their bones sticking out" with scarce any flesh, a wretched sight, coming up after the fat ones and standing beside them; the witnesses note the fitness, that these lean kine, as the interpretation would show, figured the seven coming years of grievous famine that would follow the plenty; the witnesses note the standing of the two sets together "upon the brink of the river," the fat and the lean side by side, foreshowing how the famine would follow hard upon the plenty in the same land; the witnesses note that God set the warning of the famine in the dream alongside the promise of the plenty; so that the seven lean kine rise, the figure of famine; ill favoured and leanfleshed; and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river. The witnesses note seven other kine came up after them out of the river, ill favoured and leanfleshed. The witnesses note and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river.

The witnesses note the seven lean, ill-favoured kine rising after the fat — gaunt and starved — the figure of the coming years of famine; the fat and lean standing side by side, foreshowing famine following hard upon plenty.

So the witnesses make the verse the lean kine, figure of famine. The lesson the expositors draw is that the seven lean kine rising after the fat figured the seven coming years of famine, set beside the plenty. The witnesses note the seven lean kine: "seven other kine... ill favoured and leanfleshed" — Gill notes gaunt and starved; the seven lean kine; the gaunt; the starved; the lean; the ill-favoured. The expositors observe the came up after: "came up after them" — following the fat; the came up after; the after; the the fat; the following; the the lean. The witnesses press the bones sticking out: their bones sticking out, a wretched sight; the bones sticking out; the bones; the wretched; the scarce flesh; the starved. The expositors draw the lesson on the figure of famine: the figure of the years of famine; the figure of famine; the the famine; the seven years; the grievous; the figured. The witnesses observe the side by side: the fat and lean side by side; the side by side; the the fat; the the lean; the the brink; the together. The expositors note the famine after plenty: famine following hard upon plenty; the famine after plenty; the the famine; the the plenty; the follows; the the same land. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seven other kine came up after them out of the river ill favoured and leanfleshed, the and stood by the other kine upon the brink of the river, and the lean kine. The verse thus records the lean kine, figure of famine — teaching that the seven lean kine figured the coming years of famine, set beside the plenty.
4:
"And the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up the seven well favoured and fat kine. So Pharaoh awoke." The witnesses note the lean kine devouring the fat, and Pharaoh awaking. Gill notes it seemed in the dream very strange and surprising that animals should devour one another, and that the lean should eat the fat. The witnesses note the lean kine eating up the seven fat kine, and Pharaoh awaking; the startling close of the first dream, the famine swallowing up the plenty — that the dream reaches its alarming end: "the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up the seven well favoured and fat kine. So Pharaoh awoke"; the witnesses note the strangeness of the sight, that "the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up" the fat — a thing against nature, kine devouring kine, and the lean swallowing the fat; the witnesses note the meaning the interpretation would unfold, that the years of famine would "eat up" and consume all the plenty of the years before, so that the abundance should be wholly devoured and forgotten (v.30-31); the witnesses note the force of the figure, that the famine would not merely follow the plenty but swallow it utterly, leaving no trace; the witnesses note that "Pharaoh awoke," startled by so strange a dream, its strangeness fitting it to trouble his spirit and prepare the way for Joseph; so that the lean kine devour the fat, and Pharaoh awakes; the ill favoured kine did eat up the seven fat kine. So Pharaoh awoke. The witnesses note the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up the seven well favoured and fat kine. The witnesses note So Pharaoh awoke.

The witnesses note the lean kine devouring the fat — a thing against nature — figuring the famine that would swallow up all the plenty, leaving no trace; Pharaoh awaking, startled, his spirit prepared for Joseph.

So the witnesses make the verse the famine swallowing the plenty. The lesson the expositors draw is that the lean kine eating the fat figured the famine consuming all the plenty, leaving no trace, and Pharaoh awoke startled. The witnesses note the did eat up: "the ill favoured... kine did eat up the seven... fat kine" — Gill notes a thing against nature; the did eat up; the against nature; the devour; the the fat; the the lean. The expositors observe the eat up the plenty: the famine would eat up the plenty; the eat up the plenty; the the famine; the the plenty; the consume; the swallow. The witnesses press the no trace: leaving no trace of the abundance; the no trace; the no trace; the the abundance; the forgotten; the devoured. The expositors draw the lesson on the swallow utterly: the famine would swallow it utterly; the swallow utterly; the swallow; the utterly; the the famine; the the plenty. The witnesses observe the Pharaoh awoke: "Pharaoh awoke" — startled; the Pharaoh awoke; the awoke; the startled; the the strangeness; the troubled. The expositors note the prepared for Joseph: his spirit prepared for Joseph; the prepared for Joseph; the prepared; the his spirit; the the way; the Joseph. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the ill favoured and leanfleshed kine did eat up the seven well favoured and fat kine, the So Pharaoh awoke, and the famine swallowing the plenty. The verse thus records the famine swallowing the plenty — teaching that the lean kine eating the fat figured the famine consuming all, and Pharaoh awoke startled.
5:
"And he slept and dreamed the second time: and, behold, seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good." The witnesses note Pharaoh's second dream of the seven good ears on one stalk. Charles Ellicott notes the seven ears upon one stalk, a description suited to Egyptian corn, which often bears many ears on a single stem. Gill notes he fell asleep again quickly and dreamed another dream the same night, much like the former. The witnesses note Pharaoh sleeping again and dreaming of seven rank and good ears of corn on one stalk; the second dream, the good ears that again figured the years of plenty — that God repeats the warning under a new image: "he slept and dreamed the second time: and, behold, seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good"; the witnesses note the second vision — "seven ears of corn" growing "upon one stalk, rank and good," full and plump, a sign of rich harvest; the witnesses note the fitness Ellicott marks, that the many ears "upon one stalk" suits the corn of Egypt, which often bears several ears on a single stem, so the image was drawn again from Egypt's own fields; the witnesses note that these good ears, like the fat kine, figured the seven years of plenty, the dream doubled to confirm and to press the same meaning; the witnesses note that God gave the dream a second time and in a second form that the thing might be established and Pharaoh the more moved to seek its meaning; so that the seven good ears rise, again the figure of plenty; seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good. The witnesses note he slept and dreamed the second time. The witnesses note seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk, rank and good.

The witnesses note the second dream — seven rank and good ears on one stalk — again figuring the years of plenty; the image drawn from Egypt's own corn, the dream doubled to confirm and press the same meaning.

So the witnesses make the verse the good ears, again the figure of plenty. The lesson the expositors draw is that the seven good ears, like the fat kine, figured the years of plenty, the dream doubled to confirm it. The witnesses note the dreamed the second time: "he slept and dreamed the second time" — Gill notes the same night; the dreamed the second time; the the same night; the again; the the second; the like the former. The expositors observe the seven ears: "seven ears of corn came up" — the good ears; the seven ears; the the ears; the of corn; the good; the rank. The witnesses press the one stalk: "upon one stalk" — Ellicott notes Egyptian corn; the one stalk; the Ellicott; the Egyptian corn; the many ears; the one stem. The expositors draw the lesson on the rank and good: "rank and good" — full and plump; the rank and good; the full; the plump; the rich harvest; the plenty. The witnesses observe the figure of plenty: again the figure of plenty; the figure of plenty; the the plenty; the the years; the figured; the like the kine. The expositors note the doubled to confirm: the dream doubled to confirm; the doubled to confirm; the doubled; the confirm; the established; the the meaning. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he slept and dreamed the second time, the seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk rank and good, and the good ears. The verse thus records the good ears, figure of plenty — teaching that the seven good ears figured the years of plenty, the dream doubled to confirm it.
6:
"And, behold, seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind sprung up after them." The witnesses note the seven thin ears blasted by the east wind. Gill notes the east wind is very fatal to corn, to dry, burn, and blast it, especially to the corn in Egypt, blowing from the desert of Arabia. Adam Clarke notes the blasting east wind, the scorching simoom. The witnesses note seven thin ears, blasted with the east wind, springing up after the good ears; the second part of the second dream, the blasted ears that figured the years of famine — that the blight follows the good: "seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind sprung up after them"; the witnesses note the imagery — "seven thin ears," meagre and empty, "blasted with the east wind," scorched and withered by that burning wind from the desert which the commentators note was so deadly to Egypt's corn; the witnesses note the fitness, that these blasted ears, like the lean kine, figured the seven coming years of famine, when the corn should fail and the land be parched; the witnesses note the cause of famine in the figure, the "east wind" (the scorching simoom from Arabia) that dried and blasted the grain, a fit emblem of the years when the Nile and the harvest should fail; the witnesses note that the second dream, like the first, set the famine after the plenty; so that the seven blasted ears rise, again the figure of famine; seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind sprung up after them. The witnesses note seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind. The witnesses note sprung up after them.

The witnesses note the seven thin ears, blasted by the scorching east wind from the desert — figuring the years of famine; the deadly wind a fit emblem of the years when Egypt's corn should fail and the land be parched.

So the witnesses make the verse the blasted ears, figure of famine. The lesson the expositors draw is that the seven thin, blasted ears, like the lean kine, figured the years of famine when the corn should fail. The witnesses note the thin ears: "seven thin ears" — meagre and empty; the thin ears; the meagre; the empty; the thin; the the ears. The expositors observe the blasted: "blasted with the east wind" — scorched and withered; the blasted; the scorched; the withered; the the wind; the dried. The witnesses press the the east wind: Gill notes the east wind deadly to Egypt's corn; the the east wind; the the wind; the deadly; the the corn; the the desert. The expositors draw the lesson on the figure of famine: the figure of the years of famine; the figure of famine; the the famine; the the years; the figured; the the corn fail. The witnesses observe the the simoom: Clarke notes the scorching simoom; the the simoom; the Clarke; the the simoom; the scorching; the from Arabia. The expositors note the famine after plenty: the famine set after the plenty; the famine after plenty; the the famine; the the plenty; the after; the the second dream. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seven thin ears and blasted with the east wind, the sprung up after them, and the blasted ears. The verse thus records the blasted ears, figure of famine — teaching that the seven blasted ears figured the years of famine when the corn should fail.
7:
"And the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears. And Pharaoh awoke, and, behold, it was a dream." The witnesses note the thin ears devouring the full ears, and Pharaoh awaking to find it a dream. Gill notes it appeared to Pharaoh in his dream, very amazing to behold, how it should be that thin ears should devour full ones. The witnesses note the seven thin ears devouring the seven full ears, and Pharaoh awaking to find it was a dream; the close of the second dream, the famine again swallowing the plenty, and Pharaoh's waking — that the second dream ends as the first: "the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears. And Pharaoh awoke, and, behold, it was a dream"; the witnesses note the parallel — that, as the lean kine ate the fat, so "the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears," the blasted corn swallowing up the good, an amazing and unnatural sight; the witnesses note the same meaning pressed twice, that the years of famine should consume all the plenty of the good years; the witnesses note Pharaoh's waking, "and, behold, it was a dream" — that he perceived it had all been a dream (or the second a dream like the first), and was left troubled by the strange and doubled vision; the witnesses note that the two dreams, alike in meaning, were given to confirm the certainty of the thing and to move Pharaoh to seek the interpretation; so that the thin ears devour the full, and Pharaoh awakes to a dream; the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears. And Pharaoh awoke. The witnesses note the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears. The witnesses note And Pharaoh awoke, and, behold, it was a dream.

The witnesses note the thin ears devouring the full — the famine again swallowing the plenty — and Pharaoh waking to find it a dream; the two dreams alike in meaning, given to confirm the thing and move Pharaoh to seek its interpretation.

So the witnesses make the verse the second dream's close, parallel to the first. The lesson the expositors draw is that the thin ears devouring the full, like the lean kine, figured the famine consuming the plenty, the doubled dream confirming the thing. The witnesses note the thin devoured the full: "the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears" — Gill notes an amazing sight; the thin devoured the full; the amazing; the unnatural; the devoured; the the full. The expositors observe the parallel: as the lean kine ate the fat; the parallel; the the lean kine; the the fat; the the same; the meaning. The witnesses press the consume the plenty: the famine consume all the plenty; the consume the plenty; the the famine; the the plenty; the consume; the the good years. The expositors draw the lesson on the it was a dream: "Pharaoh awoke... it was a dream" — he perceived a dream; the it was a dream; the perceived; the a dream; the awoke; the troubled. The witnesses observe the doubled to confirm: the two dreams to confirm the thing; the doubled to confirm; the two dreams; the confirm; the the certainty; the the thing. The expositors note the move to seek: to move Pharaoh to seek the meaning; the move to seek; the move; the to seek; the the interpretation; the the way. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the seven thin ears devoured the seven rank and full ears, the And Pharaoh awoke and behold it was a dream, and the second dream's close. The verse thus records the second dream's close — teaching that the thin ears devouring the full figured the famine consuming the plenty, the doubled dream confirming the thing.
